title-group.article-title: Dynamicity Predicts Inferred Temporal Order in Complex Sentences: Evidence from English, German, and Polish
title-group.alt-title: E. Marx, O. Iwan, E. Wittenberg

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence should be sent to Eva Wittenberg, Central European University, Language Comprehension Lab, Quellenstr. 51, 1100 Vienna, Austria. E‐mail: wittenberge@ceu.edu

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: To build an accurate mental model of complex situations, people infer temporal order from sometimes underspecified linguistic information. The basis on which these inferences are drawn is an open question. While previous literature has focused on the role of linguistic structure and discourse pragmatic strategies as important contributors to temporal inferences, here we argue that, under uncertainty, people also use the dynamic properties of the described situations to derive temporal order from language. In three pre‐registered studies using English, German, and Polish, adult participants used toys to act out complex situations described by main clause‐relative clause structures. We consistently find that non‐dynamic state descriptions are temporally ordered first, if the other clause describes a dynamic event. This pattern arises independently of whether dynamicity differences are lexically encoded, like in English or German, or grammatically encoded, like in Polish. More generally, our findings address an important gap in the discussion on the role of eventuality type for temporal inference. While there is substantial research on the significance of telicity and durativity, a third, much more overlooked feature is dynamicity, a concept rooted in event perception, not language. Our results therefore provide a crucial thread to closely weave together language comprehension and event cognition.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: When people hear a story, they build a mental representation of what happened—a so‐called situation model (Barsalou, 1999; Glenberg, Meyer, & Lindem, 1987; Johnson‐Laird, 1983; van Dijk & Kintsch, 1983; Zwaan, 1996; Zwaan, Magliano, & Graesser, 1995). One important dimension of such models is when something happens (Radvansky, Zwaan, Federico, & Franklin, 1998; Rinck, Hähnel, & Becker, 2001; Speer & Zacks, 2005; Therriault & Raney, 2007; Zwaan, 1996): For instance, The vet sat in the truck reports a situation in the past, while The vet will hop onto the truck tells about a situation in the future. These statements also differ in terms of other temporal properties: Sitting usually takes longer than hopping, and it describes a static, temporally homogeneous situation rather than a dynamic, punctual change‐of‐state.

More complex situations, however, consist of multiple situations with different temporal properties; and while understanding how these situations are temporally related is crucial (Briner, Virtue, & Kurby, 2011; Dery & Bittner, 2016; Gouldthorp, Katsipis, & Mueller, 2018; Mandler, 1986), it is not always straightforward: A description such as The vet sat in the truck onto which the bison hopped could either mean that the hopping event happened before the vet sat on the truck, or that the vet sat on the truck first and then the bison hopped on it.

In this article, we investigate how people understand the temporal order of complex situations like the one just described. We focus on the dynamicity of situations, like the difference between hopping and sitting, as one crucial but under‐researched component in how people construe complex temporal relations from linguistic descriptions. In the discussion on eventuality distinctions, dynamicity is the third component in conjunction with telicity/boundedness and duration/durativity, which we do not consider in the present paper. The notion of dynamicity relates to the presence or absence of change within situations in the world and is the basis for the linguistic distinction between events and states (Dowty, 1986; Kearns, 2000; Olsen, 1994). As such, it is not purely a linguistic concept but rather one arising out of event perception.

Based on previous work both from linguistics and event cognition research, we expect that dynamicity differences strongly guide the construal of situation models (Dowty, 1977; Gennari & Poeppel, 2003; Katz, 2003; Michaelis, 2011; Parsons, 2002; Pettijohn & Radvansky, 2016; Radvansky & Copeland, 2010; Rinck & Weber, 2003; Zwaan et al., 1995; Zwaan, Radvansky, Hilliard, & Curiel, 1998) and ask: When linguistic descriptions lack explicit information about the temporal relations between individual situations, how do people determine which is ordered before the other?

In three experiments, we obtain a reconstruction of people's situation models, using an act‐out task in which participants recreate complex sentences with toys. Our results show that event dynamicity, that is, whether a situation is described as a static state or as a dynamic event, is a reliable cue to temporal order: Across three languages (English, German, and Polish), we find that stative descriptions are consistently ordered before eventive descriptions. This holds true despite potential differences in temporal interpretation and linguistic encoding of dynamicity between languages.

sec.title: Encoding temporal relations in language
sec.p: In language, there is “a wide variety of strategies for describing the structure of time” (Radvansky & Zacks, 2014, p. 72): Temporal information can either be encoded grammatically through morphological tense or aspect (e.g., The vet sat/will sit/is sitting on the truck) or lexically via time adverbs (e.g., yesterday, today, tomorrow) or temporal connectives (e.g., before, after, while). In the case of complex situation descriptions, the temporal order between individual events can therefore be part of the linguistic meaning itself (1), when a connective or a relational adverb directly signals how two events are temporally linked, or the temporal relation must be inferred from the absolute temporal status of each event (2) (Evers‐Vermeul, Hoek, & Scholman, 2017; Kehler, 1994).

However, the order in which events within complex situations occur need not be specified: Extreme examples are tenseless languages (e.g., Mandarin, Dyirbal, Yucatec, Kalaallisut, cf. Bittner, 2005; Bohnemeyer, 2009; Comrie, 1985; Tonhauser, 2015), where temporal status can be marked lexically, but this marking is not mandatory (Lin, 2003; cf. Mucha, 2012; Smith, 2008). Even in languages with obligatory tense marking, a linguistic description may not provide sufficient information to establish the order between events: This occurs, for example, in discourse such as (3), in which two past tense sentences follow each other (Hinrichs, 1986; Hopper, 1979; Horn, 2022; Marx & Wittenberg, 2024; Schmerling, 1975). Temporal relations are also underspecified in past‐under‐past relative clauses such as (4), where a main clause and a relative clause each describe a situation in the past tense, both involving the relativized object (i.e., truck).

In such cases, it is up to the comprehender to infer the temporal order between the two described subevents. The crucial question, then, is: What guides these temporal order inferences? Here, we focus on relative clause constructions, whose possible temporal interpretations we further explore in the following.

sec.title: Temporal interpretations in relative clauses
sec.p: Relative clauses are an excellent phenomenon for testing temporal order inferences because, like other subordinate clauses, they link two propositions together in one utterance. However, unlike other complex constructions, relative clauses do not systematically trigger causal, temporal, or conditional inferences between the main clause and the relative clause situation.1 Formal linguistic accounts have explained this lack of systematic inferences with the structural status of relative clauses (e.g., Abusch, 1997; Diessel, 2005; Enç, 1987; Kusumoto, 1999; Ogihara, 1995; Stowell, 2007; Von Stechow, 1995; von Stechow & Grønn, 2013a, 2013b): Since relative clauses are adjuncts and thus optional constituents in the syntactic structure, comprehenders should in principle be able to interpret the tensed predicate in the relative clause temporally independently of the tensed predicate in the main clause. For (4), as long as both the sitting and the hopping took place in the past, any logical possibility of temporal order should be a computable temporal interpretation: The bison could have hopped onto the truck either before, simultaneously with, or after the vet sat on it.

At the same time, further formal analyses of relative clauses have pointed out that speakers and hearers adhere to the discourse pragmatic function of the linguistic structure (Cinque, 2020; Fox & Thompson, 1990; Lambrecht, 1988; Levinson, 1983; Quirk, Greenbaum, Leech, & Svartvik, 1985; Stockwell, Schacter, & Partee, 1973; Thomson & Martinet, 1985): Given that relative clauses either restrict the space of potential referents (i.e., restrictive relative clauses) or simply predicate the antecedent head noun (i.e., non‐restrictive relative clauses), they often only provide background information against which a main clause event unfolds; and although backgroundedness is not necessarily temporal, there seems to be a connection between pragmatic background and temporality (Hopper, 1979; Wårvik, 2004). Consequently, under such a view, comprehenders should tend to locate a relative clause description before a main clause description: In (4), the bison should have hopped onto the truck before the vet sat on it.

In general, however, temporal construal is also affected by other factors such as iconicity, whereby the order of mention maps iconically onto the order of events in the world. This is often the case in linguistic discourse (Fleischman, 1990; Hopper, 1979; Horn, 2022; Kaiser, 2019; Kamp & Rohrer, 1983; Lascarides & Asher, 1991, 1993; Ohtsuka & Brewer, 1992; Schmerling, 1975; Tai, 1983; Xu & Kwok, 2019) but also in complex sentences: Non‐linear temporal adjunct clauses pose a challenge of comprehension for both children (Blything & Cain, 2016; Blything, Davies, & Cain, 2015; Clark, 1971; de Ruiter, Theakston, Brandt, & Lieven, 2018; Trosborg, 1982) and patients with language impairments (Abrahamsen & Gilbert, 1990; Natsopoulos et al., 1991; Sasanuma & Kamio, 1976). They also cause processing costs for adults (Clark & Clark, 1968; Jou & Harris, 1990; Münte, Schiltz, & Kutas, 1998; Ohtsuka & Brewer, 1992; Van Der Meer, Beyer, Heinze, & Badel, 2002; Xu & Kwok, 2019). If iconicity restricts the temporal interpretation in sentences like (3) or (4), then comprehenders should arrange the reported situations according to the order of mention: The bison should have hopped onto the truck only after the vet had sat on it.

In sum, the temporal relation between main and relative clause is subject to several concurring, and perhaps competing, linguistic factors. In addition, we argue that one important factor in construing temporal order is dynamicity of the described situations.

sec.title: Dynamicity and temporal construal: Previous research
sec.p: In building situation models, language comprehension and event cognition interact (Berman & Slobin, 1994; Bunger, Papafragou, & Trueswell, 2013; Bunger, Skordos, Trueswell, & Papafragou, 2016; Carroll & von Stutterheim, 2010; Coll‐Florit & Gennari, 2011; Gerwien & von Stutterheim, 2018; Gleitman & Papafragou, 2012; Madden & Zwaan, 2003; Magliano & Schleich, 2000; Misersky, Slivac, Hagoort, & Flecken, 2021; Radvansky et al., 1998; Sakarias & Flecken, 2019; Santin, van Hout, & Flecken, 2021; Strickland et al., 2015; Ünal, Ji, & Papafragou, 2021; Wittenberg & Levy, 2017; Zwaan & Madden, 2005; Zwaan et al., 1995). When it comes to the temporal dimension of situations, the majority of studies has focused on how people derive the temporal properties of simple situations from factors such as boundedness or duration (Barner, Wagner, & Snedeker, 2008; Becker, Ferretti, & Madden‐Lombardi, 2013; Bott, 2010; Bott & Gattnar, 2015; Coll‐Florit & Gennari, 2011; Flecken & Gerwien, 2013; Foppolo, Bosch, Greco, Carminati, & Panzeri, 2021; Magliano & Schleich, 2000; Milczarski, 2021; Minor, Mitrofanova, & Ramchand, 2022; Paczynski, Jackendoff, & Kuperberg, 2014; Piñango, Winnick, Ullah, & Zurif, 2006; Radvansky et al., 1998; Sakarias & Flecken, 2019; Strickland et al., 2015; Wittenberg & Levy, 2017; Zwaan & Madden, 2005; Zwaan et al., 1995). However, as outlined above, relating two or more situations temporally is crucial for constructing accurate situation models of complex situations.

In previous work (Marx & Wittenberg, 2022, 2025), we therefore studied people's interpretations of past‐under‐past relative clauses like (4), to test predictions from accounts that closely link temporal construal to dynamicity (Carroll & von Stutterheim, 2010; Klein, 1994, 2009; von Stutterheim, Carroll, & Klein, 2003): According to such theories, each tensed verb encodes a specific time, a topic time, at which a situation takes place. Topic times are then linked to a reference point, a so‐called temporal anchor, and the relationship between the two is determined by tense meaning: For simple past, the anchored topic time should begin before the temporal anchor, but never after it.

In the simplest cases of situation descriptions (e.g., The vet sat on the truck), temporal anchors relate to the utterance time. In more complex situation descriptions, however, they are associated with the most salient elements mentioned in the description; therefore, they may be chosen on the basis of what comprehenders know about the structure of the encoded events. But how is saliency determined? One suggestion comes from perception and memory research, which has demonstrated that dynamic changes‐of‐state (i.e., events) are cognitively more salient than stable states (Clewett, Gasser, & Davachi, 2020; Kurby & Zacks, 2008; Zacks, Speer, Swallow, Braver, & Reynolds, 2007), with a corresponding distinction in the linguistic domain (Dowty, 1977; Gennari & Poeppel, 2003; Katz, 2003; Levin & Hovav, 1994; Michaelis, 2011; Olsen, 1994; Parsons, 2002; Pettijohn & Radvansky, 2016; Radvansky & Copeland, 2010; Rinck & Weber, 2003; Zwaan et al., 1995, 1998): There is a long tradition in linguistics of distinguishing between the different verb and predicate types on the basis of their syntactic and semantic behavior, and one central—while sometimes overlooked—feature, along with telicity and durativity, is the presence or absence of dynamic change (see Table 1, following Olsen, 1994) In language, events—whether or not they have duration or an inherent endpoint—are states with added dynamicity.

caption.p: Linguistic features to distinguish between different types of verbs or predicates, following Olsen (1994)

sec.p: Event‐structural accounts of language comprehension take this distinction between dynamic and static situations in language and cognition as a starting point to predict that eventive descriptions, corresponding to more salient changes in the world, should serve as temporal anchors, and that stative descriptions, corresponding to less salient states, should serve as anchored situations.

Our previous studies confirmed the event‐structural predictions in English and German tasks (Marx & Wittenberg, 2022, 2025, see also Marx & Wittenberg, 2024, as an extension to discourse comprehension): For past‐under‐past relative clauses, stative descriptions (i.e., sit) were generally preferred to happen before eventive descriptions (i.e., hop), regardless of whether they were encoded in the main or the relative clause and regardless of whether the eventive predicate encoded an inherent endpoint or was unbound.2 That is, whether situations were linguistically framed as a state or as an event systematically predicted people's temporal interpretations of sentences. Durativity, as the third dimension of linguistic event structure, only affected interpretations in the absence of dynamic change (Marx & Wittenberg, 2024): Longer states were ordered before shorter states.

Crucially, previous studies were mainly concerned with collecting baseline data to investigate the role of non‐linguistic event cognition in linguistic temporal construal: In Marx and Wittenberg (2022, 2025), participants watched one of two video clips in which the temporal order between two situations was manipulated, and rated on a 1–5 Likert scale whether a relative clause construction correctly described the visual scene. However, temporal construal in language comprehension may not be adequately captured by explicit acceptability judgments (for a general criticism of acceptability judgments, see Kepser & Reis, 2005; Schütze & Sprouse, 2013): One concern is that participants were presented with possible temporal interpretations in the videos without the need to actively construe a mental representation of the described scene. Furthermore, the videos only showed two clearly sequential situations, without any potential overlap. In a strict sense, our previous results may therefore only speak to how people match linguistic descriptions to visual temporal sequences in the world, not to how they model the temporal aspects of a complex situation from language.

A second and related concern is the interpretation of gradient levels of acceptability (Schütze & Sprouse, 2013; cf. Barsalou, 1987; Bresnan, 2007; Keller, 2000): In other words, what does it mean to rate a sentence with “3,” in between “1” (unacceptable) and “5” (acceptable)? In our case, the gradient results may have reflected the strength of participants’ temporal inferences, based on their event knowledge, but they may also have resulted from alternative factors: Armstrong, Gleitman, and Gleitman (1983), for example, found that even clearly categorical concepts such as “even number” elicited gradient judgments to different category exemplars, suggesting that participants reinterpreted the task as judging how representative or typical particular numbers are for a concept. In our previous studies, acceptability judgments may have also been driven by the frequency or prototypicality of particular temporal interpretations, or by the context of the visual materials we used, reflecting participants’ metalinguistic knowledge or post hoc explanations due to the task design.

Finally, in the previous studies, we focused on English and German—two languages that are somewhat different in terms of temporal interpretations in other sentence constructions but similar in the way dynamicity is linguistically encoded: Whether a described situation is stative or dynamic is part of the lexical meaning of the predicate. It is therefore an open question to what extent grammatically marking dynamicity results in a similar pattern of temporal construal.

sec.title: The current studies
sec.p: To address these questions and to test the effects of dynamicity on temporal construal more directly, the current study uses an act‐out task adopted from studies like Thothathiri and Snedeker (2008) or Wiese, Öncü, Müller, and Wittenberg (2020), to tap into people's mental construal of temporal order in language comprehension. The rationale is that temporal construal should be directly reflected in the way people act out a complex situation description: That is, those parts of the situation that are understood to have happened first in time should also be enacted first in the task. By using an act‐out task, we thus did not ask participants to explicitly reason about temporal relations, while still being able to obtain the temporal dimension of their situation model. It should be noted that act‐out tasks have been used in developmental studies to investigate temporal comprehension of complex sentences (Amidon & Carey, 1972; Bauer & Mandler, 1992; Clark, 1971; Gorrell, Crain, & Fodor, 1989; Winskel, 2004).

As laid out above, formal accounts would predict that comprehenders are able to interpret the embedded description temporally independent of the main clause description, so no particular temporal order should stand out in any of the sentences in (5): Participants should perform at chance level (i.e., enact an equal number of relative clause and main clause descriptions first), assuming that they do not take other factors into account.

If, however, people's temporal construal of past‐under‐past relative clauses is systematically determined by the function of relative clauses in discourse, this should lead to relative clauses being enacted first. Another factor could be iconicity, leading to enactments that primarily follow the linear order of the sentence. Crucially for both pragmatic strategies, we would expect no differences with respect to the different dynamicity manipulations in (5): People should follow the same strategy independently of situation dynamics.

Alternatively, event dynamicity may determine the way in which event order is derived from temporally underspecified descriptions, as suggested by previous data on acceptability judgments. In this case, participants should identify more salient eventive descriptions as the temporal anchor of less salient stative descriptions in past tense sentences and construe event sequences accordingly: Stative descriptions should be enacted first, before eventive descriptions, regardless of whether they are encoded by the relative clause (5a) or the main clause (5b). In cases where dynamicity differences do not serve as a cue to temporal order (5c,d), we would expect participants either to perform at chance level or to use one of the pragmatic strategies mentioned above (e.g., relative clauses first, linear order).

We conducted three pre‐registered experiments to test these predictions, one in English (Experiment 1), one in German (Experiment 2), and one in Polish (Experiment 3): Participants heard past‐under‐past relative clause descriptions as in (5), which were crossed in dynamicity (stative vs. eventive description) of the main and the relative clauses. After each description, participants enacted the complex situation with toys.

The three languages were chosen to investigate the role of dynamicity in temporal construal across languages that are (a) different with respect to temporal interpretations and (b) different in the way in which dynamicity is encoded (see Table 2 for a summary).

caption.p: Overview of the languages tested in each experiment, including their language‐specific properties of interest and the example sentences for each condition (1–eventive/stative, 2–stative/eventive, 3–eventive/eventive, 4–stative/stative)

sec.p: Temporal interpretations differ in English, German, and Polish with regard to the so‐called sequence of tense (SoT) phenomenon, which emerges for temporal interpretations in embedded contexts: In complement clause constructions (e.g., The vet said that the bison stood on the truck), an embedded past tense can have non‐past meaning, in addition to signaling anteriority, such that it may be interpreted simultaneously with the time denoted by the matrix clause. English is a clear case of an SoT language (Abusch, 1997; Declerck, 1988; Ogihara, 1995; Stowell, 2007), and German has likewise been classified as such (Boskovic, 2012) but, to the best of our knowledge, a systematic formal and empirical analysis is lacking (see Giorgi & Pianesi, 2000; von Stechow, 2005, for alternative assessments). In contrast to English, SoT ambiguities in German complement clauses can be resolved by optional subjunctive marking (Eckardt, 2021; Helbig & Buscha, 2001). Polish, on the other hand, is a non‐SoT language and expresses simultaneity between an embedded tense and the main clause only by embedding the present tense (Arregui & Kusumoto, 1998; Bittner, 2014; Sharvit, 2014; but see Mucha, Renans, & Romoli, 2023). It should be noted that such SoT ambiguities only arise with embedded stative predicates, so that speakers of SoT languages may be more aware of dynamicity as a cue to temporal interpretation.

In terms of dynamicity, both English and German encode differences between eventive and stative situations as part of the lexical meaning of the predicate, but English can also mark the ongoingness of an event grammatically with the progressive aspect (Parsons, 1989). Polish, on the other hand, has rich tense and aspect morphology but only optional lexical distinction between states and events (cf. Kozłowska‐Raś, 1987, for marking within aspectual classes); information about dynamicity is mainly derived from obligatory grammatical aspect marking (Bogdan & Sullivan, 2009; Comrie, 1976; Czochralski, 1975; Klimek‐Jankowska & Błaszczak, 2020; Młynarczyk, 2004; Vater, 1995).3

In sum, these language differences allow us to test two potential generalizations: The first is whether the effects of dynamicity extend beyond English and German, which are frequently ambiguous in terms of temporal construal of embedded stative descriptions (Generalization 1). The second is whether the linguistic level at which dynamicity differences are marked mediates the effects of dynamicity seen in previous studies (Generalization 2).

sec.title: General materials and procedure
sec.p: Participants were tested in a within‐subjects design: They were seated at a table with a set of four toys (mostly Playmobil) placed in a basket in front of them (see Fig. 1). Three of the toys served as critical items (e.g., vet, bison, truck), and a fourth served as a distractor item (e.g., plant). At least two of the critical toys were animate (e.g., a human agent or an animal), the third was, in some cases, and the fourth in all cases, an object artifact (e.g., plant, truck).

caption.p: Example of a basket (upper right) and a corresponding object set.

sec.p: On each trial, participants listened to a sentence (e.g., The vet sat in the truck onto which the bison hopped). After the sentence ended, they squeezed a rubber ducky with their non‐dominant hand, to emit a squeak, and then they enacted the complex situation as described. Each enactment required manipulating the two critical animate items (e.g., vet, bison) relative to the third location item (e.g., truck). The rubber ducky served to prevent any enactment before the sentence end. In addition, it forced participants to enact the sentences with one hand only. This, in turn, made the temporal dimension of their mental construals more prominent since it forced a sequential act out of either the relative clause or the main clause. We reasoned that, if the temporal order was either irrelevant or flexible according to participants’ mental models, we should find chance‐level performance across trials.

Sentences were pre‐recorded using the Google Cloud Text‐to‐Speech Application Programming Interface (API) to keep intonation as neutral as possible across conditions. The linguistic materials for each experiment are described individually in the following subsections.

We used nine different sets of toys corresponding to the experimental blocks: To familiarize participants with the toy objects, they matched the toys with their corresponding name cards at the beginning of each block (see Fig. 1). First, a practice block with three practice trials illustrated the task and the experimental procedure. The practice block was followed by eight experimental blocks, each consisting of 12 trials: one critical, one attention check (i.e., describing a temporally unambiguous sequence of events), and two filler trials, as well as eight trials for an unrelated study (published in Marx, Iwan, & Wittenberg, 2023).

The experimental blocks, as well as the trials within each block, were presented in a pseudorandomized order. For counterbalancing, participants were randomly assigned to one of four lists using a Latin square design. All experiments were programmed and live‐coded in PsychoPy; sessions were recorded on video.

sec.title: General analysis
sec.p: We performed two types of preregistered statistical analysis (cf. analysis plan at https://osf.io/qgnfr/): First, we fitted a linear mixed‐effects logistic regression model (i.e., glmer from the lme4 package; Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015) in R (R Core Team, 2014). Model significance was assessed with likelihood ratio tests, comparing the full model to reduced models that individually excluded fixed predictors and interactions, but included the same random effects structure (Barr, Levy, Scheepers, & Tily, 2013). In both experiments, the two fixed contrast‐coded predictor variables were dynamicity in the main clause (stative vs. eventive) and the combination of dynamicity in the sentence (mixed: stative‐eventive/eventive‐stative vs. same: eventive‐eventive/stative‐stative). Participants and items were included as random intercepts to control for variance possibly induced by these two factors. We used random intercepts, but no random slopes, to avoid overfitting of the regression model (i.e., minimal intercept‐only structure). Our dependent measure was which of the two clauses (main vs. relative clause) was enacted first (see Fig. 2). Second, a series of planned pairwise comparisons was conducted to assess the interaction of the regression model.

caption.p: Staged example trial of a target sentence, either enacting the main clause first (up) or the relative clause first (down).

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Forty‐nine native English speakers were recruited via the Central European University recruitment portal. We included participants in the data analysis only if all trials were completed and the criterion of at least five out of eight attention control trials was met. Based on this criterion, one participant was excluded from the study, resulting in 48 complete datasets as preregistered.4 As we only included correctly and unambiguously performed trials in the statistical analysis (e.g., involving only toys mentioned), 8.3% of all critical trials were excluded.

sec.title: Linguistic materials
sec.p: For each toy set, we constructed four critical sentences, all of which were active, intransitive past‐under‐past relative clause constructions, which described two agents relative to a location. Descriptions were either eventive (e.g., sit down, hop, climb) or stative (e.g., sit, was, stand) and were crossed between main and relative clauses.5 In two of the critical sentences, dynamicity therefore differed between the main and relative clauses (eventive‐stative 6a, stative‐eventive 6b) and, in the other two, it was uniform between clauses (eventive‐eventive 6c, stative‐stative 6d). All linguistic material consistently used the simple past.

As attention control, we included relative clause constructions that contained temporal adverbs to disambiguate between different temporal orders (e.g., previously, first/then), as well as before‐/after‐clause constructions, which forced temporal sequence due to the temporal connective (four trials per construction type). All remaining trials consisted of simple sentences. Linguistic materials can be found at https://osf.io/qgnfr/.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Participants enacted 93.2% of the attention control trials correctly, demonstrating both that the task was easy to perform, and interest did not decline over the course of the trials.

Participants’ first enactments are shown in Fig. 3: Overall, when sentences encoded both an eventive and a stative situation (pink plots), participants were more likely to enact stative situations first, regardless of whether it was in the relative clause or main clause. When clauses encoded situations of the same dynamicity (green plots), main clauses tended to be enacted first for sentences that described two eventive situations, but performance was at chance for sentences that described two stative situations.

caption.p: Mean first enactments of main or relative clause in Experiment 1 with native English participants. Dashed line depicts chance‐level performance. Figure illustrates the examples in (6), in which the vet appeared as subject of the main clause; first enactments of the vet figure therefore correspond to the first enactment of the main clause. The bison, in contrast, appeared in the relative clause; more first enactment of the bison therefore corresponds to more first enactment of the relative clause. The width of the violin plot corresponds to the means by participants; dots represent overall means, and error bars are the standard error of the mean.

sec.p: For statistical analysis, we coded relative clause first enactments as 1, and main clause first enactments as 0. We found a main effect of dynamicity in the main clause (Df = 1, χ2 = 55.74, p < .001): When main clauses contained a stative description, they were more likely to be enacted first (meanMCstative = 0.35, SD = 0.48), compared to when they contained an eventive description (meanMCeventive = 0.65, SD = 0.48).

Furthermore, there was a main effect of dynamicity combination (Df = 1, χ2 = 9.50, p = .002): Participants were more likely to enact main clauses first when clauses encoded the same dynamicity (meansame = 0.45, SD = 0.50) than when they encoded both stative and eventive situations (meanmixed = 0.55, SD = 0.50).

Crucially, we found a significant interaction (Df = 1, χ2 = 124.84, p < .001), which, according to pairwise comparisons, was driven by differences both between the two mixed dynamicity conditions (β = 0.40, t = 17.92, p < .001) and between the two conditions that encoded the same dynamicity (β = ‐0.09, t = −2.54, p = .01): When dynamicity differed between main and relative clause, participants were more likely to enact the relative clause first when the main clause was eventive (meanmixed_MCeventive = 0.95, SD = 0.21, first line in Fig. 3), but they preferred to enact the main clause first when it was stative (meanmixed_MCstative = 0.15, SD = 0.36, second line in Fig. 3). In cases where dynamicity matched between clauses, participants were more likely to enact the main clause first when the sentence reported two eventive situations (meansame_MCeventive = 0.35, SD = 0.48, third line in Fig. 3) than when it described two stative situations (meansame_MCstative = 0.54, SD = 0.50, last line in Fig. 3).

sec.title: Discussion Experiment 1
sec.p: In Experiment 1, we asked how people model the temporal relations of past‐under‐past relative clauses and whether the dynamicity of the described situations would be used to guide inferences. When systematically manipulating dynamicity in both the main and relative clauses, we found that people have a clear tendency to place the stative description first in time—at least when the other described situation is an event.

However, differences in dynamicity were also reflected between conditions in which dynamicity was the same across clauses: Participants had a weak preference to follow the iconic order of mention, that is, the temporal order in which they learned about the situations in the sentence, when both described situations were events. For stative‐stative descriptions, this preference disappeared completely, as reflected in participants’ chance‐level performance. This pattern of results is in line with the observation in linguistics, that events, not states, move a narrative in time (Dry, 1982; Hopper, 1979; Partee, 1984).

sec.title: Experiment 2: German
sec.p: In Experiment 2, we tested the effects of dynamicity encoding on people's temporal construal in German—a language that, like English, is typologically classified as an SoT language (but see Giorgi & Pianesi, 2000; von Stechow, 2005), but with less robust temporal ambiguity for embedded stative predicates: additional grammatical marking of mood can remedy SoT ambiguities (Eckardt, 2021; Helbig & Buscha, 2001).

Importantly for our purposes, however, German shares one important feature with English: Dynamicity is part of the lexical predicate meaning. We reasoned that if lexically encoded dynamicity is a reliable cue to temporal order across languages, not only a resolution strategy unique to English, we should obtain a similar pattern of results as in Experiment 1 with German‐speaking participants.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: We recruited 48 native German speakers via the Central European University recruitment portal. As in Experiment 1, datasets were only submitted to data analysis if they were complete and met the criterion of performing five out of eight attention control trials correctly. No participant was excluded from the analysis based on this criterion, but we excluded 3.4% of the critical trials due to incorrect performance (e.g., enactments with toys that were not mentioned).

sec.title: Linguistic materials
sec.p: As in Experiment 1, we used active, intransitive past‐under‐past relative clause constructions, describing either the movement or the posture of one of the toy agents toward or at a location. Main and relative clauses were systematically manipulated with respect to their encoded dynamicity (eventive vs. stative), resulting in two critical sentences that encoded different dynamicity types (7a, b) and two critical sentences that encoded uniform dynamicity across clauses (7c, d).

We used the German preterite as the historical and structural equivalent tense of the English simple past (Comrie, 1995; Helbig & Buscha, 2001; Klein, 2000). Furthermore, to keep semantic contrasts to a minimum, we used a German‐specific predicative contrast: stative posture predicates (i.e., stehen “stand,” sitzen “sit,” liegen “lie”) were contrasted with their transitive counterparts (i.e., stellen “put,” setzen “place,” legen “lay down” see De Knop, 2016; Fagan, 1991; Hermann, 2019).

Filler trials were simple sentences, and attention checks used an equal number of temporally unambiguous relative clause constructions (i.e., temporal adverbs vorher schon “previously,” zuerst/dann “first/then”) and bevor‐/nachdem‐clause constructions (i.e., four trials per construction type; linguistic materials can be accessed via https://osf.io/qgnfr/).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In 93.5% of cases, the attention control was performed correctly. Participants thus understood the task and remained attentive throughout the experimental session.

Like in Experiment 1, participants were more likely to enact stative descriptions first when the other clause encoded an eventive situation (Fig. 4, pink plots). This was the case regardless of whether the stative situation was encoded by the relative clause or the main clause. Unlike in Experiment 1, however, relative clauses were generally more likely to be enacted first, when the main and relative clauses did not differ in their encoded dynamicity (green plots).

caption.p: Mean first enactments of main or relative clause in Experiment 2 with native German participants. Dashed line depicts chance‐level performance. Figure illustrates the examples in (7), in which the bird appeared as the subject of the main clause; first enactments of the bird figure therefore correspond to the first enactment of the main clause. The boy, in contrast, appeared in the relative clause; more first enactment of the boy therefore corresponds to more first enactment of the relative clause. The width of the violin plot corresponds to the means by participants; dots represent overall means, and error bars are the standard error of the mean.

sec.p: For statistical analysis, we again coded relative clause first enactments as 1, and main clause first enactments as 0. The statistical analysis revealed a main effect of dynamicity in the main clause (Df = 1, χ2 = 52.15, p < .001), but no main effect of dynamicity combination (Df = 1, χ2 = 0.13, p = .72): Participants were more likely to first enact stative main clauses (meanMCstative = 0.45, SD = 0.50) than eventive main clauses (meanMCeventive = 0.77, SD = 0.42), but on average, performances were similar between conditions where the event types differed (meanmixed = 0.56, SD = 0.50) and conditions where they were the same across clauses (meansame = 0.65, SD = 0.48).

Crucially, the interaction between the two predictor variables was significant (Df = 1, χ2 = 61.10, p < .001), resulting from differences between the mixed dynamicity conditions (β = 0.34, t = 12.94, p < .001): Here, relative clauses were enacted first when main clauses encoded eventive situations (meanmixedMCeventive = 0.91, SD = 0.28, first line in Fig. 4), but not when they encoded stative situations (meanmixedMCstative = 0.23, SD = 0.42, second line in Fig. 4). In cases where dynamicity was matched between clauses, participants generally preferred to enact the relative clause first (meansameMCeventive = 0.64, SD = 0.48, third line in Fig. 4, vs. meansameMCstative = 0.67, SD = 0.47; β = −0.02, t = −0.50, p = .62, last line in Fig. 4).

sec.title: Discussion Experiment 2
sec.p: In Experiment 2, we aimed to understand whether effects were due to any property specific to temporal interpretations in English. We replicated the main finding of Experiment 1 in German: When one clause described a state and the other an event, participants reliably ordered the state before the events, regardless of whether it was encoded in the main or relative clause. In other words, dynamicity differences predicted the temporal order in participants’ enactments. However, there was also a difference between the pattern of results in Experiments 1 and 2: When dynamicity was uniform across clauses, German participants were less sensitive toward dynamicity differences between conditions and showed an overall preference to enact relative clauses first. We will return to this cross‐linguistic difference in Section 6.

In both Experiments 1 and 2, we showed that the lexical distinction between dynamic events and non‐dynamic states reliably predicts temporal construal in past‐under‐past relative clauses. Other languages, however, use different means to express the same distinction: Slavic languages are a well‐known case of marking aspect morphologically on the verb, encoding not only the speaker's perspective on a situation but also how it is temporally and dynamically constituted. We therefore conducted Experiment 3 in Polish—a non‐SoT language for which no temporal ambiguity is reported for other embedded contexts (Mucha, Renans, & Romoli, 2023).

sec.title: Experiment 3: Polish
sec.p: Experiment 3 investigated whether the effects of dynamicity on people's temporal construals would extend to languages that (a) are non‐SoT languages and thus show no temporal ambiguity in other embedded contexts and (b) mark the distinction between events and states grammatically rather than lexically: In Polish, the perfective aspect most commonly refers to complete events, but especially in the past tense, it refers to specific events in terms of momentary occurrences, without reference to their internal temporal structure. The perfective aspect thus refers to dynamic activities that involve an instantaneous change in the event situation (Bogdan & Sullivan, 2009; Comrie, 1976; Czochralski, 1975; Młynarczyk, 2004; Vater, 1995). In this sense, situations described by the perfective aspect coincide with lexically encoded events.

Polish imperfective aspect, on the other hand, indicates a lack of completion or change within a described event situation and is often associated with habitual or durative states or processes as well as narrative background (Czochralski, 1975; Comrie, 1976; Vater, 1995; Młynarczyk, 2004; Bogdan & Sullivan, 2009). Thus, the imperfective aspect seems to have similar dynamicity properties as lexically encoded states.

We predicted that regardless of the linguistic level at which a language marks the conceptual distinction between events and states, dynamicity should drive people's understanding of temporal sequence in language comprehension: In Experiment 3, as in the previous experiments, we expected stative situations, conveyed by imperfective aspect, to be enacted first.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: As preregistered, a total of 36 native Polish speakers were recruited Central European University recruitment portal, local language tandem cafes, and online language exchange forums. All participants met the attention control criterion and performed at least five out of eight control trials correctly. Less than 1% of trials were excluded due to incorrect performance.

sec.title: Linguistic materials
sec.p: Critical sentences matched the materials used in Experiments 1 and 2: past‐under‐past relative clause constructions, describing either a dynamic movement or a static position of two toy agents, with the encoded dynamicity crossed between main and relative clauses: Two critical sentences encoded with different event types in the main and relative clause (8a, d) and two critical sentences encoded the same (8b, c).

We employed the contrast between perfective and imperfective past to systematically mark different dynamicity properties: Perfective forms such as usiadła (“sat”) corresponded to lexically encoded events, and imperfective forms such as siedziała (“was sitting”) corresponded to lexically encoded states.

Attention checks matched the relative clause constructions in the critical trials but established an unambiguous temporal order of events, using temporal adverbs (see linguistic materials at https://osf.io/qgnfr/).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Performance accuracy was at 97% in all attention control trials. As in the previous two experiments, participants were more likely to enact stative situations (conveyed by imperfective aspect) first rather than eventive situation (conveyed by perfective aspect; Fig. 5, pink plots). When there were no differences in aspectual marking (green plots), performance was at chance for sentences that encoded two eventive (i.e., perfective) situations, but relative clauses tended to be enacted first for sentences that encoded two stative (i.e., imperfective) situations.

caption.p: Mean first enactments of main or relative clause in Experiment 3 with native Polish participants. Dashed line depicts chance‐level performance. Figure illustrates the examples in (8), in which the swimmer appeared as the subject of the main clause; first enactments of the swimmer figure therefore correspond to the first enactment of the main clause. The chicken, in contrast, appeared in the relative clause; more first enactment of the chicken therefore corresponds to more first enactment of the relative clause. The width of the violin plot corresponds to the means by participants; dots represent overall means, and error bars are the standard error of the mean.

sec.p: Again, for statistical analysis, we coded relative clause first enactments as 1 and main clause first enactments as 0. The main effect of dynamicity in the main clause (Df = 1, χ2 = 15.16, p < .001) but not of dynamicity combination (Df = 1, χ2 = 0.69, p = .41) was significant: Eventive main clauses were less likely to be enacted first (meanMCeventive = 0.65, SD = 0.48), compared to stative main clauses (meanMCstative = 0.46, SD = 0.50).

Like in English and German, we found a significant interaction (Df = 1, χ2 = 50.09, p < .001). Pairwise comparisons revealed that the interaction was driven by differences both between the two mixed dynamicity conditions (β = 0.29, t = 8.34, p < .001) and between the two conditions that encoded the same dynamicity (β = −0.09, t = −2.23, p = .03): With different event types in the main and relative clauses, participants enact more often the relative clause first when the main clause encoded an event (meanmixed_MCeventive = 0.82, SD = 0.39, first line in Fig. 5) than a state (meanmixed_MCstative = 0.24, SD = 0.43, second line in Fig. 5). With the same dynamicity across clauses, participants were at chance level for double‐eventive sentences (meansame_MCeventive = 0.49, SD = 0.50, third line in Fig. 5), but they were more likely to enact the relative clause first for double‐stative sentences (meansame_MCstative = 0.68, SD = 0.47, last line in Fig. 5).

sec.title: Discussion Experiment 3
sec.p: Experiment 3 investigated whether structural differences in events also serve as a cue to the temporal order of events in non‐SoT languages, and when differences are encoded by grammatical means. Overall, we replicated the pattern of results from English and German in Polish—a language without temporally ambiguous state descriptions in other embedded contexts (Mucha et al., 2023) and where aspectual marking expresses not only viewpoint differences but also the dynamic structure of events (Bogdan & Sullivan, 2009; Comrie, 1976; Czochralski, 1975; Młynarczyk, 2004; Vater, 1995): For sentences where both a stative and an eventive situation was described, using imperfective and perfective marking, respectively, participants enacted the stative description before the eventive description. The syntactic encoding of the state in the main or relative clause was irrelevant.

Like in Experiment 1, but unlike in Experiment 2, we also found a significant effect of dynamicity between conditions that encoded the same event type, though the nature of this effect differed: Performances were at chance level when participants learned about two eventive situations, but similar to the overall preference of German speakers in both conditions, relative clauses tended to be enacted first when sentences described two states. These differences will be discussed in the following section.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: To investigate how the mapping of event onto temporal structure varies across languages, we conducted an exploratory analysis: We pooled the data from Experiments 1 (English), 2 (German), and 3 (Polish) and added language to the linear regression model, using a Helmert coding scheme: English and German were coded as one Helmert pair, and contrasted with Polish as the second Helmert pair.

Across conditions, Polish native speakers did not enact sentences systematically different from English and German native speakers (meanPOLISH = 0.55 vs. meanENGLISH&GERMAN = 0.56); and English native speakers did not perform differently from German native speakers (meanENGLISH = 0.50 vs. meanGERMAN = 0.61; no main effect of language: Df = 2, χ2 = 2.85, p = .24). Fig. 6 shows the overall patterns of results as a combination of Figs. 3, 4, 5.

caption.p: Mean first enactments of main or relative clause across languages (combined Figs. 3–5). Dashed lines depict chance‐level performance, width of violin plots corresponds to means by participant, and error bars denote standard errors.

sec.p: However, we found a significant interaction between language and dynamicity in the main clause (Df = 2, χ2 = 7.45, p = .02) which, according to pairwise comparisons, was driven by differences between conditions that encoded an event in the main clause: Here, German participants were more likely to enact relative clauses first (meanGERMAN_eventiveMC = 0.77) as compared to both English participants (meanENGLISH_eventiveMC = 0.65; β = −0.58, z = −2.48, p = .01) and Polish participants (meanPOLISH_eventiveMC = 0.65; β = −0.58, z = −2.33, p = .02).

Language furthermore interacted with dynamicity combination (Df = 2, χ2 = 8.83, p = .01), such that participants performed similarly in the mixed dynamicity conditions across languages, but language differences emerged in the conditions encoding the same event type: English participants showed a weak tendency to enact main clauses first (meanENGLISH_same = 0.45), whereas relative clauses tended to be enacted first by German (meanGERMAN_same = 0.65; β = 0.84, z = 3.92, p < .001) and Polish participants (meanPOLISH_same = 0.58, β = 0.55, z = 2.44, p = .01).

Finally, the three‐way interaction was significant (Df = 2, χ2 = 9.38, p = .009), showing that language differences were more pronounced in the conditions encoding the same event type and more so when the two clauses encoded eventive situations: Polish participants performed at chance (meanPOLISH_eventive‐eventive = 0.49), whereas German participants tended to enact the relative clause first (meanGERMAN_eventive‐eventive = 0.64) and English participants tended to enact the main clause first (meanENGLISH_eventive‐eventive = 0.35).

sec.title: Discussion of between‐language comparison
sec.p: We compared three different languages to assess two generalizations: First, we asked whether linguistic encoding of dynamicity is a strong cue to temporal order across different language families, not only in SoT languages like English and German, where dynamicity is tied to temporal ambiguity in other embedded contexts. Second, we wanted to determine whether the linguistic level at which languages encode dynamicity properties—that is, lexicon or grammar—would interact with temporal construal derived from these properties.

The answer to both questions is clear: The between‐language comparison revealed that dynamicity serves as a reliable cue to temporal order in both SoT languages (English, German) and non‐SoT languages (Polish), at least when two situations differ in dynamicity.

Any differences between languages pertained to the conditions encoding the same dynamic properties, which speaks more to the question of how participants construed temporal order in the absence of, rather than on the basis of, dynamicity differences. Here, English and German speakers used different pragmatic strategies: English participants took the iconic order of mention to reflect the temporal order of the events but not the states in the world, whereas German overall adhered to the discourse pragmatic function of relative clauses, assuming that they generally encode situational background. Polish speakers patterned with German speakers but more so when enacting double‐stative sentences.

Since our focus in this paper was on the effect of dynamicity differences, we had no clear predictions about which strategy speakers of different languages should follow in the absence of such differences. However, two points are noticeable: First, the same‐dynamicity conditions in all three languages elicited comparably noisy data (green plots). Language‐specific frequency and markedness, as well as discourse‐pragmatic functions of relative clauses, may play a role here. We suggest further research to understand why speakers rely on different cues across English, German, and Polish.

Second, and more importantly, when dynamicity was indeed mixed (pink plots), the effects were clear and consistent across languages, confirming previous results in different tasks (Marx & Wittenberg, 2022, 2025) and linguistic structures (Marx & Wittenberg, 2024). That is, state‐first temporal construal seems to be a robust and reliable strategy that is employed cross‐linguistically under conditions of temporal uncertainty.

sec.title: General discussion
sec.p: In this article, we investigated whether people would infer the temporal structure of a complex situation—that is, when a situation occurred relative to another situation—from the encoded dynamic properties of its parts. We asked: In cases where language does not provide sufficient information to establish temporal order, is one described situation temporally ordered relative to another depending on its linguistic encoding as a state or an event? Furthermore, do dynamicity differences guide temporal construal across languages independently of SoT and independently of whether dynamicity is marked in a language's grammar or lexicon?

To answer these questions, we conducted three experiments in English, German and Polish—three languages that differ typologically and in terms of their tense‐aspect systems—while using a linguistic context that describes two situations without any systematic causal, temporal, or conditional link: past‐under‐past relative clauses.

In previous work on relative clause constructions, we showed that people tend to prefer stative situations to happen before eventive situations, regardless of their syntactic encoding in either the main or relative clause, and regardless of discourse pragmatic factors, such as linearity or a background‐foreground distinction between clauses (Marx & Wittenberg, 2022, 2025). However, one limitation of previous research was that participants explicitly judged how well sentences matched visual scenes. This left open the possibility that the observed preference was induced by factors other than the underlying effects of dynamicity on temporal construal, and instead related to task design (i.e., post hoc explanations, metalinguistic knowledge, see e.g., Kepser & Reis, 2005; Schütze & Sprouse, 2013). In the current series of studies, we therefore assessed temporal construal through language and independent of visual representations: Participants enacted relative clause descriptions, using toys as props, according to their own understanding of the sentences.

In all three languages, we replicated the previous pattern of results. When one of the clauses encoded an event and the other one a state, dynamicity predicted participants’ performance: Stative situations were reliably ordered before eventive situations. This was the case regardless of language‐specific SoT properties or the linguistic encoding of dynamicity. In what follows, we discuss the implications of this cross‐linguistically stable effect.

First and foremost, our findings provide further support for accounts that place event structure at the core of understanding temporal structure: In complex situation descriptions, comprehenders constantly monitor how individual subevents are linked (Kehler, 1994; Radvansky et al., 1998; Zwaan et al., 1995). In terms of temporal relations, this requires them to temporally anchor each described situation by another time or situation (Carroll & von Stutterheim, 2010; Klein, 1994, 2009; von Stutterheim et al., 2003). Although comprehenders may use utterance time as a temporal anchor, event‐structural accounts predict that they are more likely to choose another, more salient situation in more complex descriptions. What our findings show is that one factor that seems to be a robust determinant of this choice is dynamicity: Dynamic events are cognitively more salient than stable states (Clewett et al., 2020; Kurby & Zacks, 2008; Zacks et al., 2007); therefore, descriptions encoding dynamic events tend to serve as temporal anchors for descriptions of non‐dynamic states. Participants’ enactments were in line with these predictions.

Admittedly, our interpretation of these data rests on the level of the events themselves. An alternative explanation may be that the referents involved in the acted‐out situations were rendered more salient by virtue of participating in a dynamic event rather than a static situation, giving rise to temporal order not based on the event itself but an increase of referents’ saliency.6 On the basis of our data, we cannot decide whether the saliency introduced by dynamicity was related only to event participants or to entire situations or to both. However, we would assume that either way, these saliency differences result in temporal construal along the lines event‐structural accounts propose. By contrast, if our data reflected only saliency differences (especially for event referents) without temporal construal, our intuitive prediction would be that the most salient referent—that is, the dynamic event participant—should have been enacted first, in line with previous work on agent saliency (Dowty, 1991; Goldin‐Meadow, So, Özyürek, & Mylander, 2008; Hafri, Trueswell, & Strickland, 2018; Mayrhofer & Waldmann, 2014; Verfaillie & Daems, 1996) and not second, as it was the case in our data.

Second, our results show that temporal inferences, driven by dynamicity, arise independently of how differences are encoded in a given language: Participants ordered stative descriptions before eventive descriptions when structural differences between events were rooted in the lexicon, as in English (Experiment 1) and German (Experiment 2), but also when the difference between states and events was grammatically marked, as in Polish (Experiment 3). While this should be confirmed by large‐scale cross‐linguistic research, our findings are a first attempt to test the generalizability of the effects of dynamicity on temporal construal.

Third, our findings target an important gap in the discussion on lexical and grammatical aspect (cf. Bertinetto & Delfitto, 2000; Binnick, 1991; Carlson, 1981; Comrie, 1976; de Swart, 2012; Depraetere, 1995; Dowty, 1977, 1979; Filip, 2019; Garey, 1957; Moens, 1987; Moens & Steedman, 1988; Mourelatos, 1978; Parsons, 1989; Sasse, 2002; Smith, 1986). While many studies emphasize the importance of telicity (or boundedness) and duration (or durativity) to determine aspectual class (e.g., Bertinetto & Delfitto, 2000; Comrie, 1976; Depraetere, 1995; Dowty, 1979; Filip, 2019; Lyons, 1977; Mourelatos, 1978; Pustejovsky, 1991; Verkuyl, 1972), to drive temporal inferences (Becker et al., 2013; Flecken & Gerwien, 2013; Milczarski, 2021; Wittenberg & Levy, 2017), and to understand inferences of event completion, repetition, and individuation (Barner et al., 2008; Bott, 2010; Bott & Gattnar, 2015; Foppolo et al., 2021; Minor et al., 2022; Paczynski et al., 2014; Piñango et al., 2006), dynamicity is a third, much more overlooked component of aspect in research on temporal construal (Olsen, 1994).

Overall, our results indicate that manipulating dynamicity leads to consistent temporal inferences across three languages. One may speculate that the reason why dynamicity has been less interesting for linguistic research is that it is perceptually grounded: Unlike telicity, which is an inherently linguistic feature, susceptible to grammatical and lexical devices, it is part of event knowledge whether a situation changes or not. In perception, dynamicity is immediately evident; one might even speculate that it is less penetrable by linguistic encoding. We leave this question to future research at the interface of language and event cognition.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The present series of experiments demonstrates that, without specific information about temporal order, comprehenders resolve the lack of specification in complex linguistic descriptions by using the dynamicity properties of a sentence as a cue. In three act‐out experiments, conducted in English, German and Polish, participants consistently preferred arranging non‐dynamic states prior to dynamic events. Our results thus confirm findings from previous acceptability judgment studies, by probing the mental construal of temporal sequences. Overall, our studies provide further insights into the underlying mechanisms of event comprehension, suggesting that, across languages, event knowledge is at the heart of understanding temporal relations in language.

sec.title: Conflict of Interest Statement
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

sec.title: Open Research Badges
sec.p: This article has earned Open Data and Open Materials badges. Data and Materials are available at https://osf.io/qgnfr/.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We thank Attila Balla for his support in data collection; Gergely Csibra, Jesse Snedeker, Alper Demircan, and Natalia Jardon Perez for their critical comments and review; and, of course, Julius and Nils for providing their toys for scientific purposes.

fn.p: Relative clauses may imply causal, or at least, coherence relations in discourse processing (Hoek, Rohde, Evers‐Vermeul, & Sanders, 2021; Rohde, Levy, & Kehler, 2011), but previous studies used implicit causality predicates, which have been shown to elicit causal biases independently in other linguistic constructions (Hartshorne & Snedeker, 2013; Solstadder & Bott, 2022).
The distinction between telic and atelic predicates was part of an exploratory analysis as part of the pre‐registration at https://osf.io/6ae5m/.

As Table 2 shows, we have not included any SoT language in which dynamicity is grammatically encoded because, to the best of our knowledge, there is no language that has SoT properties but no inherent lexical aspect. For example, in Romance languages such as French, an SoT language, the indication of dynamicity is linked both to the lexical meaning of predicate and to the tense‐aspect system (imparfait vs. passé simple/composé). Here, we focus on the basic question of what role dynamicity plays in temporal construal, leaving for future research the question of how lexical encoding of dynamicity interacts with tense and aspect marking in triggering temporal inferences.

We preregistered between 36 and 48 participants per experiment, based on previous act‐out studies (Thothathiri & Snedeker, 2008; Wiese, Öncü, Müller, & Wittenberg, 2020) with a maximum sample size of 35. Based on the effect size of the interaction between event type combination and event type in the main clause, the observed power was at 100.0% for all three experiments (CIs: [92.89, 100.0]; effect sizes via likelihood ratio >1.0).

Since previous results have found no difference between bounded/unbounded eventive descriptions tasks (Marx & Wittenberg, 2025) and an effect of duration only in the absence of dynamic differences (Marx & Wittenberg, 2024), we have left aside questions about the interaction with other event‐structural dimensions such as telicity and durativity and focused only on dynamicity in linguistic encoding.
We thank an anonymous reviewer for this suggestion.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: All data, code, analyses, and linguistic materials are accessible at https://osf.io/qgnfr/.
